speaker: interviewer
text: How long did you spend in a labor battalion?

speaker: interviewee
text: From memory, I would think about two weeks perhaps. And it wasn't too bad. I regarded this as a holiday. The food was good. The [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] camaraderie was good friends, there and so forth. But I was unhappy when they took us to the other side of the Danube because, naturally, I wanted to stay where the Russians were coming. So I didn't stay around. They asked for volunteers. They deposited of us in the basement of a brewery. I didn't like the place at all. That would have been about 30 kilometers south of Budapest. And they asked for volunteers for something or other to go outside. And I volunteered, and I just kept on walking without looking back. And I kept on walking until I got back home. As I said, that would have been well over 30 kilometers. And I do remember that when I crossed the Danube, German engineers were fitting explosive charges to the bridges then. Nobody raised a finger, I might at.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when you returned home, did you still have your false papers with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes, yes. Well, look, you used a false paper as you had to. Obviously, you needed some where to live. And that wasn't easy. It was lucky that our house was a designated Jewish house. Even though that had its difficulties, but I fixed it up beautifully so that I could get out of the place whenever I wanted to. And outside, of course, you had to use false papers. Inside, you had to use a Schutzpass or a-- a whatever. At that time, as I said, my Schutzpass was no longer any good. I had to get out of the place in a hurry. The way I solved it was, on top of the house, there was a-- a-- lift the house. And I could get out onto the flat roof. And I made a key to the lock of our-- of our house, our roof. And I made a key for the roof of the next house, which was a Christian place. So I could get out of the place no trouble at all any time without asking anybody.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you ever use that escape route?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many times did you use it?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think only once.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about that time.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I fixed up a place when the Gestapo came checking on top of the lift. The lift was stuck. It wouldn't work anymore. And I thought nobody would think of looking there. But for some reason or other, I couldn't get into it. So I got right out of the place.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I don't remember where I went. But later on, you sort of had a routine. Ridiculous as it may sound today, we went to the public baths with a friend of mine. We stayed there for a couple of hours. Then we had breakfast, if we could get it. And just-- just hung around. The streets were safer than-- than inside. Although it was becoming difficult because as time went on, I was 17 at the time, and a fairly tall 17. They were calling up 15-year-old kids in to the army. And I was getting worried that-- I adjusted my papers accordingly. But nevertheless, it was getting dicey.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you adjust your papers?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, with the method I mentioned, you washed out one lot of ink and put in another.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any other Schutzpasses besides the Swiss one?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Describe to me how you got them.

speaker: interviewee
text: That was a beauty. Now, that was very close to the-- to the end of it all. It must that been January '45. And Budapest, at that stage, was surrounded by-- by the Russians damn near three months. And nothing came in or out. And incidentally, that was the reason why they killed the Jews locally. They couldn't take them out anymore. When the Germans had no transport to transport Jews in cattle wagons, the Arrow Cross would start marching them off towards the border, you know, emptying the ghetto. And march off all people towards the border,

speaker: interviewer
text: Which border?

speaker: interviewee
text: Most of them, to the Austrian border. And most of them, of course, died on the way. But some did get to Mauthausen that way. I'm sorry. I lost the question. You were going to tell me about Schutzpasses that you had. Yes, well, look, so it was nearly the-- the end. And there was shooting going around, shooting, bombing, you name it. It was really quite an awful situation. It was bad to be a Jew in Budapest. But to be non-Jew in Budapest was also no great shakes because the bombs didn't differentiate. And at that time, there was artillery shelling all over the place and from every direction. And a friend of mine and myself we heard that--

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes, yes, I keep contact with him to this day. He lives in Bratislava, and his name is Strasser, George. And we heard that if you turn up at the Spanish embassy, they give you a Schutzpass. And because the Spaniards were fascists, the same as the Germans, the Spanish Schutzpass counted for something. The Swiss Schutzpass was reasonable. The Swedish Schutzpass was reasonable. Nothing what they make out today that Wallenberg saved hundreds and thousands of Hungarian Jews. That's a lot of load of garbage. It saved a Jew for a day, but that was life. All Schutzpass worked like that. And in the ranking of the Schutzpasses, I would say the Spanish was the best. In for instance, the Vatican, a Schutzpass wasn't worth the paper it was written on. And so we started off to walk to the Spanish embassy. And we had to walk across the railway yards. There was a bridge leading over the railway yards. And miraculously, it was still not a shot fired. Until we got to the other side, and then all hell broke loose. I forget-- oh yeah, mortars. They started-- you know, mortars mean that the Russians really were just a kilometer or so away. Because they don't go very much longer, further. But the noise they make is unbelievable, and it puts really the fear of God into you. Particularly, when you see people going up 100 foot in the air and disappearing, vaporizing. And we did get to the Spanish embassy nevertheless. And remember--

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember what street the Spanish embassy was in?

speaker: interviewee
text: Look, I can be sure I remember that it was in a little street parallel to the boulevard that was going around Budapest, the [NON-ENGLISH]. I think it was [NON-ENGLISH]. But I'm not all together sure. I haven't been there before or to that day since. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Who told you where it was?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Look when you're trying to survive, nobody needs to tell you any of these things, you know. These things are in the air. You've got to have all your wits about you to live another day and then another one after that.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we arrived at the embassy. And outside, it was war. Inside, it was peace. That was incredible. It was quiet inside. And I still remember sumptuous carpets and antique furniture.

speaker: interviewee
text: And there was a secretary there. And we told her what we wanted. And she said, won't be a minute. And took us into the ambassador's office. Imagine, the ambassador himself, sitting right behind this desk, and as friendly and sympathetic as you like. And so we asked him if he-- if he would give us a Schutzpass. And he said, yes, no trouble at all. It will only take a few minutes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember it now. I didn't know it at the time. I didn't know that he wasn't even a Spaniard. He was an escaped Italian prisoner of war. And I didn't find this out until 20 years after the war. His name was Giorgio Perlasca, and the man couldn't even speak Spanish. Now, we spoke to him through an interpreter. But I couldn't, at that time, I don't think I could have told you the difference between Spanish and Italian. I'm not sure if I can tell the difference today. And he was really terrific. I remember him apologizing that he was very sorry that he can't put us up in a Spanish protected house because they were chockablock full. However, he gave us some meal tickets that we could cash at a Spanish kitchen in a Spanish house. And well, we parted on note. Shook hands with him, and we thought he was a terrific guy.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did he look like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Look, I really can't describe him to you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember what color hair he had?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Or how tall he was? Was he fat or thing?

speaker: interviewee
text: Look, I'd be guessing. I'd be guessing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was he wearing a uniform?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, he was well-dressed, a beautiful suit. I accepted him as the ambassador, yeah. Now, what actually happened was that the Spanish embassy packed up weeks before when-- before the Russians surrounded the city. And went back to Spain. And this bloke, somehow or other, reopened the embassy and set himself up in business. Saved himself as well as must have saved dozens, perhaps hundreds, of Jews. And we arrived at the Spanish house to cash his meal tickets. By then, we were very hungry. There was no food anywhere. And [NON-ENGLISH] I do remember that a young Arrow Cross-- I honestly don't think he was more than 14-- chasing two Jews with a bucket down the stairs into the cellar. Because water pressure by then was so poor, that you could get water only in the cellar. And you know, for one second I thought to myself, I grab that rifle off that little bastard then kill him. But the moment passed. I hesitated too long. And I regret it to this day, because without a doubt, he'd have killed those two Jews. But that's-- that's one of the regrets. Anyway, that's-- that was the flavor of the-- of the situation then.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you went to the Spanish safe house, and you had a meal there.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And after that, look, we-- we stayed wherever we could. Slept in ruins, all over the place. It was a very cold winter. So it was no picnic.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you return at all to your apartment in [INAUDIBLE]?

speaker: interviewee
text: Only at the very end when there was no more. For the last three four days, the Arrow Cross were bottled up in their house. And they were fighting for their life then, and they left the Jews alone. But that wouldn't be more than two or three days. Between times, I lived in all, stayed, and slept in all sorts of places, all over the place.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you know what was happening to your mother during that period?

speaker: interviewee
text: I kept in touch with her.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was she?

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, my mother was in the best possible racket. She had a Christian friend who lived in a Christian flat in a Spanish protected house. There was nothing better than that. And I got her false papers, not a lot, but enough. My mother didn't look a bit Jewish, so quite happily, they could go down to the cellar. And her friend introduced her to everybody, and no problems at all. She was OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: And she stayed there until the end of the war?

words.text: until the end of the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: She stayed there until the end, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Did you hide in anybody else's apartment or house?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do-- can you describe of those incidences for me?

speaker: interviewee
text: Look, I remember staying perhaps for a week, four or five days, in a house. It was a suburban type house on the way out of the city towards the outer suburbs. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] an old lady put us up.

speaker: interviewer
text: You and George?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, that was another friend.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: His name was Spiegel, also Joseph.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is he still alive?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, he's died. And he survived the war, all right, but he died after. And I think it might have been an acquaintance of his. And we were quite happy staying there waiting for the Russians to arrive because it was on the way to the front. And then one day, a German engineer came and said that they were blowing up the road right in front of the house, and we better get out. He didn't know, of course, we were Jewish. So we thought we'd better shift.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you shift to?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, god knows, I really can't remember where we went from there.

speaker: interviewer
text: At any stage, did you witness yourself any roundups or actions?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you describe one to me?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, look, the way it would happen, the Gestapo with the help of Hungarian police would come and round up Jews from a Jewish house, from a protected house, wherever. And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] they were reasonable compared to the Arrow Cross. The Hungarian police, they were not political. The gendarmes, they were mongrels.

speaker: interviewer
text: The gendarmes were Arrow Cross?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, the gendarmes were the police force set up, I imagine, originally to terrorize the peasantry in the country. And they had the reputation that they would kill their grandmother if-- if they were ordered to.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the gendarmes were from the country?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were [INAUDIBLE] from the country.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the Hungarian police were from Budapest?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the cities, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they were reasonable. And look, I remember Hungarian police escorting Jews to the ghetto. I saw that every day. And the Jews, of course, would always want to carry more than they could actually carry. And as they were being escorted, reasonably tolerantly by the police, they would, of course, drop all their possessions because they just couldn't carry them anymore. Most of them were old. And you know, that's one of those disgusting memories. When I see the vultures from the side street-- sidewalk, moving in and picking up the bits and pieces that the Jews dropped. Quite a bit of gloating at the same time-- well, you know, you think they ought to have known better, but they didn't. That was one side of the story. The other side were the Arrow Cross. Now, whoever they could lay their hands on, they killed. And it-- it would happen. For instance, at a round up of the Gestapo with Hungarian police, the Arrow Cross would lurk on at the edges. Where the police weren't looking, they would grab a Jew. Kill him on the spot. They were much happier walking them down to the Danube and killing them there. You know, that's-- you could say it's a funny thing that to this day, that's always a problem of murder as, you know, to dispose of bodies. And when they started in this business, and I think as I said before, most of these Arrow Cross were children of 14 and 15. They would kill them on the banks of the river, and then toss them in the river and let them float to wherever. And the Danube froze over fairly early in the peace. So they couldn't chuck the bodies into the water. So what they did was they piled up the bodies in neat piles like fire wood. It's funny, we were refreshing our memories with my friend in Bratislava a year ago.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he was wondering the sort of mind who would kill people, and then neatly make a pile out of human bodies three this way, three that way. And without any exaggeration, that pile would've been close on a kilometer long. Can you imagine that? Where was this pile?

speaker: interviewee
text: And sort of something like six feet high, as fire-- as far as they could lift the bodies I imagine. That pile was at the banks of the Danube. There was a sort of double bank, a lower one and an upper one. It was on the upper bank. And I got to see that pile the first day the Russians came in. You didn't really take a walk down there while the Arrow Cross were still around. And by then, the pile was covered in snow, but you could still see the yellow stars. Without exception, they were Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know anybody-- or did you ever see anybody who was a gendarme or Hungarian policeman that you knew before the war began or before the Arrow Cross took control?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, but the only one I knew by reputation was a priest whose name I couldn't remember anymore. Maybe it was [NON-ENGLISH]. I'm not-- I'm not sure though. He was a priest with a yellow Arrow Cross armband on. And with a Tommy gun in his neck, and his business was killing Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever see him?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't actually see him kill a Jew, but he was in the business all right. It was easier for them to walk the Jews to the Danube and kill them there than having to cart them there. Yeah, it's part of the story that he was caught. And he was tried. He was also defrocked by the-- by the Vatican. And they hanged him after the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did the roundups ever come into your building whilst you were living there in [INAUDIBLE]?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ah, yes, ah, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And explain to me how a round up would take place when it happened in your building.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, on the first occasion, I showed a photocopy of our Schutzpass to a-- to a Gestapo officer. And he accepted it, with perhaps one question. Why haven't got-- you got the original? I told him my father has it. He accepted it no worries. I knew the second time he wouldn't accept it. I had to hide then. And the subsequent occasions if I was there, I had to get out. I knew I wouldn't get away with it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did your mother do?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother wasn't there by then.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, she wasn't there by then.

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were just living in-- in your apartment.

speaker: interviewee
text: I wasn't really living there. I was living wherever I could, you know, wherever you could grab yourself a shelter for the night.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did anybody else ever stay in [INAUDIBLE] apartment with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, look, towards the end, there must have been 20 people there, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember any of those people?

speaker: interviewee
text: Vaguely, vaguely-- relations, friends, that sort of thing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know anything about the deportations that took place of the Jews by the Nazis?

speaker: interviewer
text: From Budapest?

speaker: interviewer
text: From Budapest.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, their scope was limited by then because [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] they were either no railway connections anymore or Allied bombings were probably effective. Or they had no rolling stock. I don't know which. The only deportation from Budapest that took place was on foot by the Arrow Cross. And there was a concentration camp near Budapest that the Germans operated. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They deported. They emptied their camp many times.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name of the camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: [? Kistarcsa. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you know that that was a deportation camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: Look, everybody knew. And that camp became quite famous because-- it might have been towards June, 1944, when the Regent regained his authority, he gave out the order that no more deportation of Jews from Hungary. But of course, you've got to remember, all the Jews by then from the country were gone, deported. And Eichmann, apparently in defiance of that order, filled up a trainload with Jews from this camp and sent them off to Auschwitz. And apparently, somebody tipped off the Regent. And before the train left Hungarian territory, the Regent sent gendarmes after that train. They brought the train back to [? Kistarcsa. ?] And within three days, the people were released. Everybody knew it. And that was like a national holiday for the Jews of Budapest.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know anybody who was ever who was deported?

speaker: interviewer
text: In general, from Budapest?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Look, all of my classmates, because virtually, all of my classmates. We were going to a Jewish school. And a lot of the kids were from the country. And naturally, in times of stress, they want to be with their families. [COUGHS] Pardon me. [DRINKS] And so the first thing they did was the day that the Germans got there, they went to the nearest railway station and wanted to go home. And the Gestapo were right there waiting for any Jew who tried to catch a train. They must have studied psychology. They were taken to this concentration camp. And within days, they were in Auschwitz. And virtually, all of my classmates were deported like this.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know anybody who was killed in a round up by Arrow Cross or the Nazis?

speaker: interviewee
text: One friend, one friend who was killed by the Arrow Cross.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. We're going to finish it there.
